---
title = "Inline"
---

Inline expressions let you embed computed values directly in your prose. Wrap an expression in backticks with the engine name, and *Calepin* evaluates it during rendering.

# Syntax

Write `` `{r} expr` `` in your text. *Calepin* replaces it with the evaluated result. For example:

| You write | You get |
|-----------|---------|
| `` `{r} mean(1:10)` `` | `{r} mean(1:10)` |
| `` `{r} round(pi, 4)` `` | `{r} round(pi, 4)` |
| `` `{python} 2 + 2` `` | `{python} 2 + 2` |
| `` `{sh} echo hello` `` | `{sh} echo hello` |

# R formatting

Numeric results in R are automatically formatted with 3 significant digits and comma separators for large numbers:

| You write | You get |
|-----------|---------|
| `` `{r} 123456789` `` | `{r} 123456789` |
| `` `{r} pi` `` | `{r} pi` |

# Python

Python inline expressions work with any expression that produces a printable value:

| You write | You get |
|-----------|---------|
| `` `{python} 2 ** 10` `` | `{python} 2 ** 10` |
| `` `{python} "hello".upper()` `` | `{python} "hello".upper()` |

# Shell

Shell expressions capture stdout:

| You write | You get |
|-----------|---------|
| `` `{sh} echo hello` `` | `{sh} echo hello` |
| `` `{sh} date +%Y` `` | `{sh} date +%Y` |

# Using inline code with chunk results

Variables defined in code chunks are available to inline expressions. This is useful for reporting computed results in text:

```{r}
#| echo: fenced
n <- nrow(mtcars)
avg_mpg <- mean(mtcars$mpg)
```

Then in your prose, write: "The dataset has `` `{r} n` `` cars with an average fuel economy of `` `{r} round(avg_mpg, 1)` `` mpg."

Which renders as: "The dataset has `{r} n` cars with an average fuel economy of `{r} round(avg_mpg, 1)` mpg."
